coredata.dc:title: DFT study of 6-amino-3-(1-hydroxyethyl) pyridine-2,4-diol (AHP) adsorption on Coronene
coredata.dc:creator: Ullah, Zakir
Jee Kim, Hyun
Jang, Sooin
Sheena Mary, Y.
Wook Kwon, Hyung
coredata.dc:description: Adsorption of 6-amino-3-(1-hydroxyethyl) pyridine-2,4-diol (AHP) on Corenene and NNP (Nitorgens doped on para position), NBP (Nitrogen and Boron on ortho position), and NBO (Nitrogen and Boron on ortho position) doped Coronene has been studied with Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Time-dependent DFT simulations. The bond distance, adsorption energy, charge analysis, frontier molecular orbital analysis (FMO), dipole moment, AIM, RGD, UV–Vis, and density of states (DOS) along with their solvent effect has been considered while conducting this study. The nucleophilic part of the AHP act as an electron-donating and the Corenene sheet acts as an electron acceptor and resulting and intermolecular interaction vis nucleophilic and electrophilic region approach. The total Gibbs free (ΔG) adsorption energy of AHP was calculated to be −5.07, −10.51, and −11.22 kcal/mole respectively to the CC, BBP, and NNP graphene quantum dots. Moreover, the change in interaction energy (ΔE) and change of enthalpy (ΔH) were found to be −3.66 and −2.75, –22.13 and −21.44, −20.94 and −20.08 kcal/mole correspondingly. Our calculations show that AHP–NNP complex is the most stable complex among the other studies system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DFT
Adsorption
Non-covalent Interaction
UV–Vis
DOS
SERS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Density of state (DOS), Reduced density gradient(RGD) Iso-surfaces, and AIM analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Priority Research Centers Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (2020R1A6A1A03041954). “Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science & Technology Development (Project No. PJ015755022022)”. Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea and the Center for Women in Science, Engineering and Technology (WISET) Grant funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT(MSIT) under the Program for Returners into R&D (Project No. WISET-2021-377).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometry and electrostatic potential map of a) Corenene, b) para Boron doped, c) para N doped, d) N, B para doped, e) and B, N Ortho doped.
Optimized geometry of a) 6-amino-3-(1-hydroxyethyl) pyridine-2,4-diol, b) HOMO, c) LUMO, d) and electrostatic potential map.
DFT Optimized geometry of AHP and graphene and NN, BN, and BB doped graphene complexes.
HOMO – LUMO plot of Coronene, NN, BN, and BB doped graphene.
HOMO – LUMO plot of Coronene– AHP complexes a) CC-AHP, b) BBP- AHP, c) NNP-AHP, d) BNP-AHP, and e) BNO-AHP.
MEP plots of Coronene – AHP complexes a) CC-AHP, b) BBP- AHP, c) NNP-AHP, d) BNP-AHP, and e) BNO-AHP.
Calculated UV–Vis spectra of isolated AHP and Coronene.
Calculated UV–Vis spectra Corenene – AHP complexes.
AIM, RDG Iso-Surfaces, and scattered graph.
Calculated SERS spectra of a) AHP, b) CC-AHP, c) BBP- AHP, d) NNP-AHP, e) BNP-AHP, and f) BNO-AHP.
Chemical descriptors (eV) of AHP, Coronene sheets, and AHP-graphene complexes.
Mulliken charges of AHP and Coronene – AHP complexes.

head.ce:title: DFT study of 6-amino-3-(1-hydroxyethyl) pyridine-2,4-diol (AHP) adsorption on Coronene

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences and Bioengineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adsorption of 6-amino-3-(1-hydroxyethyl) pyridine-2,4-diol (AHP) on Corenene and NNP (Nitorgens doped on para position), NBP (Nitrogen and Boron on ortho position), and NBO (Nitrogen and Boron on ortho position) doped Coronene has been studied with Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Time-dependent DFT simulations. The bond distance, adsorption energy, charge analysis, frontier molecular orbital analysis (FMO), dipole moment, AIM, RGD, UV–Vis, and density of states (DOS) along with their solvent effect has been considered while conducting this study. The nucleophilic part of the AHP act as an electron-donating and the Corenene sheet acts as an electron acceptor and resulting and intermolecular interaction vis nucleophilic and electrophilic region approach. The total Gibbs free (ΔG) adsorption energy of AHP was calculated to be −5.07, −10.51, and −11.22 kcal/mole respectively to the CC, BBP, and NNP graphene quantum dots. Moreover, the change in interaction energy (ΔE) and change of enthalpy (ΔH) were found to be −3.66 and −2.75, –22.13 and −21.44, −20.94 and −20.08 kcal/mole correspondingly. Our calculations show that AHP–NNP complex is the most stable complex among the other studies system.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their unique electronic and optoelectronic properties with intriguing applications, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently received a lot of attention [1–7]. The opening of a band gap and significant photoluminescence response due to the quantum confinement effect is one of the most intriguing properties of GQDs in comparison to graphene [1–3]. It has already been established that when the size of GQDs (<10 nm) the bandgap widens [8–10]. Another aspect is the increased doping feasibility, which is especially useful for sensing and electro-catalysis applications [11–14]. Most experimental methods for obtaining GQDs result in the creation of various chemical functional groups, particularly at the edges but also at the basal place [12–14]. However, functionalization and doping of GQDs with additional elements, primarily nitrogen and sulfur, have recently been described, demonstrating exceptional modulation of the optical-electronic characteristics [15–22]. However, there is still a lot of variation in the experimental optoelectronic properties, which is most likely due to the diverse fabrication methods for these doped GQDs. Recent theoretical methodologies on DFT calculations have been developed to better understand the characteristics of GQDs and their interaction with organic molecules [23–26]. Due to their similar atomic radii to C, B, and N impurities are excellent for graphene doping. The use of B- and N-doped graphene for Raman enhancement and detection of molecules has been reported, as well as adjusting electronic characteristics of graphene to make it behave as a p-type or n-type semiconductor material [27–29]. Furthermore, the scientific community has concentrated its efforts on reducing the recovery time of graphene-based sensors so that they can be used in real-world applications [30,31]. There are a variety of well-known computational atomistic simulation methodologies that can be used to assess the adsorption and mechanical and electrical properties of various systems. The results calculated by this method for solid systems are in close agreement with experimental data, and DFT calculations provide a good estimate of the interaction mechanism and mechanical behavior [32,33]. Mashhadzadeh et al. used DFT calculations to investigate the adsorption properties of heavy metal atoms on graphene [34]. Jhi et al. employed a pseudopotential DFT to study molecular hydrogen adsorption on BN nanotubes and found a 40% increase in binding energy when compared to carbon nanotubes [35]. A number of studies based on the adsorption behavior of pharmacological compounds with graphene have recently been published in the literature by Mary et al. [36–52].

Herein, we have presented a DFT investigation of 6-amino-3-(1-hydroxyethyl) pyridine-2,4-diol (AHP) adsorption on coronene and B/N doped coronene (CC: pristine coronene; BBP/NNP: B/N atom substitution at para positions; BNP: B and N at para positions; BNO: B and N at ortho positions in the center ring of CC) are presented in this paper. Initially, the AHP is aligned parallel to the graphene at a distance (<3Å) that allows for maximum interaction and is optimized for minimum energy. The finished structure’s stability was then assessed using adsorption energies. Chemical descriptors, structural characteristics, and the density of state analyses were all calculated.

All the computations for the AHP molecule and AHP–complexes were done with Gaussian 16 software [53,54]. The B3LYP-D3 extended hybrid functional with a 6-31G* basis was utilized to determine the most stable structures. The Ead (adsorption energy) of AHP on the graphene surface was calculated as.
Ead = EComplex – EAHP – Egraphene [50,51].

Mulliken charge analysis was used to assess charge transfers between AHP and Coronene surfaces. The thermodynamic parameter’s change, Gibbs free energy (ΔG), enthalpy (ΔH), and entropy (ΔS) were studied to guarantee that all relaxed systems corresponded to the global minima on the potential energy surface.

Fig. 1 shows the most stable structure of Coronene sheets. The calculated bond lengths of the Coronenes are C-C = 1.4247 to 1.4301 Å (CC), B-N = 1.5082 to 1.5222 Å (BBP), C-N = 1.4056 to 1.4251 Å (NNP), B-C = 1.4901 to 1.5040 Å and C-N = 1.4080 to 1.4303 Å (BNP) and B-C = 1.4848 to 1.5003 Å, C-N = 1.4020 to 1.4254 Å (BNO) systems. Fig. 2 gives the optimized geometry, FMOs, and MEP plot of the drug molecule.

In drug absorbed systems (Fig. 3 ), the bond lengths are: C-C = 1.4256 to 1.4303 Å (CC-AHP), B-B = 1.5313 to 1.5471 Å (BBP- AHP), C-N = 1.4208 to 1.4381 Å (NNP- AHP), B-C = 1.4898 to 1.5046 Å and C-N = 1.4066 to 1.4301 Å (BNP- AHP) and B-C = 1.4848 to 1.5013 Å, C-N = 1.4023 to 1.4256 Å (BNO- AHP) systems. In CC, BNP- AHP, and BNO-AHP, due to adsorption, no significant change in bond length is observed. But in BBP- AHP, and NNP- AHP, B-N and C-N bond lengths show an increase due to adsorption.

AHP is interacted with CC at one edge through O21-H22 and O7-H8 at distances 2.6088 and 2.8841 Å and assumes a tilted orientation with respect to the CC. In the BBP-AHP complex, the AHP molecule is nearly parallel to the BBP surface and interaction occurs through NH2 which is at a distance of 3.7663 Å from the BBP surface. The AHP molecule assumes a nearly perpendicular orientation with the NNP surface with the O9-H 10 group near the surface at a distance of 3.0125 Å. In the BNP-AHP complex also, the AHP molecule assumes a nearly perpendicular orientation with respect to the BNP surface and interaction occurs through O9-H 10, NH2, and C2-H6 which are at distances 2.5033, 3.1670, and 2.9158 Å, respectively, from the BNP surface. The AHP molecule interacts with the edge of the BNO surface through O7-H8 and O21-H22 at distances 2.8509 and 2.4867 Å from the graphene edge with a slightly tilted orientation. The computed binding energy values for all AHP are −4.12 (CC), –23.23 (BBP), −21.75 (NNP), −6.86 (BNP) and −4.23 kcal/mol (BNO). BBP and NNP with AHP give maximum adsorption energies while CC and BNO give minimum energy which is nearly equal. For the adsorption of AHP with BNP also energy is low but greater than the lowest values [46–49]. The negative values of adsorption energy suggest chemisorption for the AHP on Coronene surfaces.

Changes in thermodynamic parameters are given in Table 1 in which all entropy changes are negative and the maximum change is for BBP-AHP and minimum change for CC-AHP. Changes in Gibbs energy and enthalpy for all systems are negative which means the processes are exothermic and spontaneous.

The bandgap (Eg), which is the energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO levels, has an inverse relationship with a compound’s electrical conductance. As can be seen, CC has a band of 4.0738 eV but when AHP adsorbs on its surface the Eg drops to 4.0531 eV. For pristine doped graphene, the Eg values are 0.3654 (BBP), 0.3442 (NNP), 2.7826 (BNP), and 2.8155(BNO) while the corresponding values due to adsorption of AHP are: 1.1916, 1.1575, 2.8055 and 2.7864 eV. For parallel doping of B and N atoms, the Eg value increases during the adsorption process which is maximum for identical substitution in the case of BBP and NNP. This suggests that Coronene’s electrical conductivity changes during the AHP adsorption process. As a result, these Coronenes may be extrapolated to be an ideal electroactive sensing material for the creation of new electrochemical sensors for determining AHP [55].

Other key chemical descriptors were calculated as well, with the findings listed in Table 2 . The reactivity of material is clearly related to its hardness and chemical potential. Indeed, molecules with lower hardness and higher chemical potential are more reactive because the crucial electron transmissions required for the execution of a chemical reaction are easier to accomplish in them [56]. Hardness and chemical potential both increase and decreases when AHP interacts with undoped and doped graphene, indicating that AHP -graphene complexes are more reactive than pure AHP. Chemisorption of AHP ring significant changes in the energies of FMOs of doped graphene [52].

Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 show the FMOs plot for pristine surfaces and AHP–Coronene surfaces. For pristine Coronene, the localizations (Fig. 1) are: In CC both HOMO and LUMO are over the carbon atoms except for the axial C and H atoms. In BBP, HOMO is mainly over B containing center ring without localization in C atoms three-ring system along vertical axis while LUMO is only on the B atoms in the center ring and outer C atoms. In NNP, HOMO is all over the C and N atoms while LUMO is not in N atoms and connected C atoms along the horizontal path. In BNP, HOMO is over the entire molecule while LUMO is also over the entire portion except for the N atom. In BNO, HOMO is over the entire ring except for the C atom para to B and LUMO is also over the entire system except for B and para C atom [50].

Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 depict the MEP plot of pristine and AHP adsorbed graphene. The MEP plot supports non-uniform charge distribution by showing the higher (positive) and lower (negative) charged area of the drug. The red to the blue color scheme of MEP indicates the electron-rich site (electrophilic attack) to the electron-deficient (nucleophilic attack) region. In the MEP plot of AHP (Fig. 2), the most reactive sites are O atoms and H atoms as given by red and blue colors. In pristine graphene (Fig. 1) for CC and BBP, red color concentrates at the center of the system, while for NNP there is a concentration of blue color at the center. In BNP and BPO, the red color is shifted to the boron substituted rings and the blue color on the N substituted regions. All of these give a non-uniform charge distribution in the pristine Coronene sheets [49]. Fig. 8.

In AHP adsorbed systems, there is again a redistribution for the reactive sites; In CC, nearly the whole C atoms become yellow while O atoms of the AHP are still red and H atoms are bluer shaded. Nearly the same distribution is seen for AHP adsorbed BBP; In AHP adsorbed NNP complex, the center of the N substituted ring potential decreases giving a white color while O atoms of the AHP become redder, and the same redistribution is seen in the BNP system also. In the AHP adsorbed BNO complex B containing ring becomes more red and yellow in nature and the AHP atoms O atoms also become more red and yellow. This redistribution of reactive sites is due to the chemisorption process and non-uniform distribution of charges as given by Mulliken’s charge analysis.

Mulliken charge analysis predicts total charge distribution among the constituent atoms in the molecules and aids in determining whether a bond is covalent or ionic. A low bond population value suggests an ionic bond whereas a large value indicates a covalent bond. A bond population value of zero indicates a perfectly ionic bond, while values greater than zero imply increasing amounts of covalency [57]. Table 3 shows the Mulliken charges for the AHP molecule and AHP adsorbed structures. The functional groups of AHP interacting with the doped/undoped Coronene changes due to adsorption as follows: For the CC-AHP complex, charges of O7, O21, and H8, H22 become more negative and positive in comparison with the AHP charge values; The charges of N11, H12 and H13 are lowered during the adsorption of the AHP on BBP; In the case of NNP-AHP adsorption charge of O9 become more negative and charge of H 10 also increase to higher positive value; In BNP-AHP system C2, O9 becomes more negative and H6, H 10 become more positive due to adsorption process; In BNO-AHP system, O7, O21 slightly increases and H8, H22 increases. Also the Mulliken charges of atoms near the interacting atoms of AHP with the graphene show changes in values (Table 3).

The UV–Vis spectra were calculated by TD-DFT calculations using 6-311G**(d, P) with the cam-B3LYP-D3 level of theory. The maximum absorption for isolated AHP was calculated to be 250 nm, however, this wavelength was greatly affected while interacting with the coronene system. After interacting with CC coronene the absorption wavelength was shifted to 275 nm, which shows the electronic charge transferring between the AHP and coronene. A similar effect was also observed for the BBP, NNP, BNP, and BNO-AHP complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density of state (DOS), Reduced density gradient(RGD) Iso-surfaces, and AIM analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The density of states (DOS) of a given system explains the fraction of states that must be taken by the system at each energy. The DOS is defined as D(E) = N E /V, where N(E)δE is the number of states in the system of volume V whose energies lie in the range from E to E + δE. The electronic structure properties and the overall DOS spectra of AHP and Corenene/NB doped Corenene – AHP were simulated at cam-B3LYP-D3/6-311G**(d,p) level of theory. These properties were calculated to investigate/explore reactivity and the extent of the interactivity of AHP with the Corenene and NB doped Coronene. The contour of occupied orbitals, virtual orbitals, and DOS plots of AHP and their complexes are shown in Fig. 9 . The bandgap is significantly affected after the interaction of AHP with Corenene and NB-doped Corenene. The most striking change is seen in the DOS spectra of the AHP – NNP Corenene complex.

Fig. 10 displays the Atoms in the Molecule (AIM), Reduced density gradient (RDG) iso-surfaces, and RGD scattered graph of AHP – Corenene/NB-doped Corinne. The numerical data for the simulated bond critical point (BCP) systems were performed on the basis of Bader‘s theory, ρ and ∇2ρ for all studied DFT cases. H-bonding rendering to AIM theory states that ρ and ∇2ρ must be in the range 0.002–0.04 a.u. and 0.020 to 0.139 a.u, respectively. The BCPs for AHP–Corenene, AHP–NNP, AHP–BNO, and AHP–BNP interaction are considered, and it was observed that AHP–NNP at BCP has the highest values of ρ and ∇2ρ, than the AHP–BNO and AHP–BNP complexes, however in the case of AHP–BNP the BCP also shows strong interaction of the doped Corenene with the AHP at three positions, one with the hydroxyl group (OH) and with the amine hydrogen of the AHP. In the RDG plots the variant ensigns of the interaction and surface spectacle the strength of the interaction and repulsion. The blue color stands for strong interaction and the green or green–brown color displays the moderate type of interaction, while the red color displays repulsive forces or ring strain.

In the AHP–Coronene complexes’ Raman spectrum (Fig. 10), it is found that very large enhancement occurs for the vibrational modes of AHP due to the adsorption process which is based on surface-enhanced Raman scattering [41]. In the case of AHP adsorption on CC, the OH stretching modes of O7-H8 and O21-H22 appear at 3495 and 3448 cm−1 with Raman intensity multiplication from 5.29 and 0.80 (normal intensity) to 5.82 and 1.91. For BBP-AHP, NH2 stretching mode at 3633 has an increase in intensity from 1.08 to 1.24, and for NNP-AHP, O9-H 10 stretching mode at 3531 possesses an intensity variation from 3.13 to 3.79. For BNP-AHP modes of O9-H 10 and C2-H6 undergo changes from 3.13 to 5.05 and 2.87 to 13.29. In BNO-AHP, the modes of O7-H8 and O21-H22 are present in the SERS spectrum at 3491 and 3448 with intensity variations from 5.29 to 6.06 and 0.80 to 3.65. Also, most of the fingerprint region vibrational modes of the AHP undergo enhancement due to the adsorption process; The normal Raman mode at 1623 of AHP with an intensity of 1.48 appears with the intensity of 16.18 in CC, 32.71 in BBP, 73.04 in NNP, 4.83 in BNP and 4.72 in BPO complexes. Similarly, the normal mode of AHP at 1576 having an intensity of 0.26 is enhanced to intensity values of 41.23 (BBP), 37.36 (NNP), 9.25 (BNP), and 12.52 (BNO) complexes. Similarly, a number of modes active and inactive in the normal Raman spectrum of AHP become active and enhanced in AHP -graphene complexes due to SERS effects.

In summary, we have conducted a theoretical study on the adsorption of AHP on Coronene surface, applying DFT and TD-DFT calculations along with 6-311G**(d,p) and B3LYP-D3 level of theory. Different parameters (FMO, DOS, AIM, RDG, SERS, etc) were utilized to understand the phenomenon of AHP adsorption on Coronene sheets. Among the studied cases, the AHP–BBP complex was found to be the most stable complex with a ΔG value of −11.22 kcal/more, ΔE of –22.13, and ΔH of 21.44 kcal/mol.

Zakir Ullah: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft, Validation. Hyun Jee Kim: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Sooin Jang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Y. Sheena Mary: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Hyung Wook Kwon: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Priority Research Centers Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (2020R1A6A1A03041954). “Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science & Technology Development (Project No. PJ015755022022)”. Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea and the Center for Women in Science, Engineering and Technology (WISET) Grant funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT(MSIT) under the Program for Returners into R&D (Project No. WISET-2021-377).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.119436.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
